{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_c2e816c284204acf8c40ca7f","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# gribtract Plan\n2\t\n3\t## Overview\n4\t\n5\tA pure-Rust GRIB2 decoder that turns NOAA/WMO GRIB2 messages into typed metadata\n6\tand gridded numeric data, gated at every step on field-by-field agreement with a\n7\treference decoder (eccodes / wgrib2) over a corpus of real NOAA products.\n8\t\n9\tThis is an **oracle-first** project. The differential harness is built before the\n10\tdecoder is \"done,\" so that autonomous (marathon) coding sessions always have a\n11\tconcrete, externally-verifiable hill to climb: *find a message gribtract decodes\n12\twrong → add it as a fixture → fix the decode → harness confirms no regression →\n13\trepeat.*\n14\t\n15\t## Design principles\n16\t\n17\t1. **The oracle is the product.** The decoder is only as trustworthy as the\n18\t   corpus and the differential check behind it. Harness work is never \"overhead.\"\n19\t2. **Real files only.** Fixtures are actual NOAA grib2 messages (GFS, HRRR, NBM,\n20\t   GEFS, …), not synthetic. An upstream archive already captures these — seed from it.\n21\t3. **Tolerance is derived, not guessed.** Grid-value equality is checked within\n22\t   the precision implied by the packing template (e.g. decimal/binary scale\n23\t   factors), not a magic epsilon. The tolerance policy is documented and tested.\n24\t4. **Every disagreement becomes a permanent fixture.** Monotonic improvement; the\n25\t   regression ratchet is the whole point.\n26\t5. **Sub-minute verify.** `cargo test` (local, cgroup-limited or CI) must run\n27\t   the full differential suite fast enough to drive an unattended loop. Large\n28\t   corpora are sharded/sampled in the fast path, exhaustive in CI.\n29\t\n30\t## Architecture\n31\t\n32\t```\n33\t                 ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n34\t   .grib2 bytes  │  gribtract-core (no_std-friendly where able)  │\n35\t   ───────────►  │                                                │\n36\t                 │  Indicator(0) → Identification(1) → Local(2)   │\n37\t                 │   → Grid Def(3) → Product Def(4)               │\n38\t                 │   → Data Representation(5) → Bitmap(6)          │\n39\t                 │   → Data(7) → End(8)                           │\n40\t                 │                                                │\n41\t                 │  section split → template dispatch → unpack    │\n42\t                 └───────────────┬───────────────────────────────┘\n43\t                                 │ Vec<Field { meta, grid, values }>\n44\t                 ┌───────────────▼───────────────┐\n45\t                 │  gribtract (high-level API)    │  iterate messages,\n46\t                 │  + CLI (`gribtract decode`)    │  select by key, dump JSON\n47\t                 └───────────────┬───────────────┘\n48\t                                 │\n49\t          ┌──────────────────────▼───────────────────────┐\n50\t          │  differential harness (tests/ + xtask)         │\n51\t          │  gribtract vs eccodes/wgrib2 over corpus       │\n52\t          │  → field-by-field + grid-within-tolerance      │\n53\t          └────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n54\t```\n55\t\n56\t### Crate layout (workspace)\n57\t\n58\t- `crates/gribtract-core` — section parsing, template dispatch, unpacking. No I/O.\n59\t- `crates/gribtract` — high-level message iterator, field selection, public API.\n60\t- `crates/gribtract-cli` — `gribtract decode|list|dump` (JSON/CSV out).\n61\t- `crates/gribtract-testutil` — shared test utilities and fixtures for differential testing.\n62\t- `crates/gribtract-py` — Python bindings via PyO3.\n63\t- `crates/gribtract-fetch` — HTTP byte-range fetching for GRIB2 files with provider probing.\n64\t- `xtask` — corpus management, reference-decoder runner, tolerance reports.\n65\t- `crates/gribtract/tests/` — differential conformance suite (differential.rs, station_extraction.rs).\n66\t\n67\t## Components\n68\t\n69\t### Section parser\n70\tSplits a GRIB2 message into sections 0–8, validates length/number fields, supports\n71\tmulti-field messages (repeated sections 2–7) and the GRIB2 \"edition 2\" framing.\n72\t\n73\t### Template registry\n74\tGRIB2's complexity lives in three template families. Each gets a registry mapping\n75\ttemplate number → decoder:\n76\t- **Grid Definition Templates (3.x)** — lat/lon, Lambert conformal, polar\n77\t  stereographic, Gaussian, rotated grids. Determines point geometry.\n78\t- **Product Definition Templates (4.x)** — what the field *is* (parameter, level,\n79\t  forecast time, ensemble member, statistical processing).\n80\t- **Data Representation Templates (5.x)** — how values are packed: simple, complex,\n81\t  complex+spatial-differencing, IEEE float, JPEG2000, PNG.\n82\t\n83\t### Unpackers\n84\tThe hard numeric core. Per data-representation template: read reference value,\n85\tbinary/decimal scale, bit-depth; expand bitmap; apply scaling; (for 5.40/5.41)\n86\tJPEG2000/PNG decode; (for 5.3) spatial differencing reversal.\n87\t\n88\t### Differential harness\n89\tRuns both decoders over the corpus, compares metadata exactly and grid values\n90\twithin derived tolerance, emits a per-template coverage + agreement report.\n91\t\n92\t### Throughput & proof-of-speed dashboard\n93\tA self-contained HTML dashboard that **proves gribtract decodes at the claimed\n94\tspeed, on real files, reproducibly** — and shows it next to correctness so speed\n95\tis never claimed without proof of a correct decode. Fed by a machine-readable\n96\t`bench-results.json` emitted by the benchmark harness (`cargo run --bin xtask -- bench`),\n97\tnever hand-edited. See `docs/notes/throughput-dashboard.md` for the full spec.\n98\tThis is the artifact that answers \"is it actually working at that speed?\" — it is\n99\ta deliverable, not an afterthought, and is wired in early (Phase 2) so every later\n100\titeration updates it.\n101\t\n102\t### Provider probe & selection\n103\t\n104\tgribtract's fetch layer (Phase 7) must retrieve GRIB2 files from one of several\n105\tupstream providers that mirror the same NOAA data with different latency and\n106\tthroughput characteristics depending on deployment location:\n107\t\n108\t| Provider | Hosts | Notes |\n109\t|----------|-------|-------|\n110\t| NOAA S3 | `noaa-gfs-bdp-pds`, `noaa-hrrr-bdp-pds`, `noaa-gefs-pds`, `noaa-nbm-grib2-pds` (all us-east-1) | Direct regional endpoint; no CDN; ~10ms from US east coast, ~100ms from Europe |\n111\t| Google Cloud Storage | `high-resolution-rapid-refresh`, `national-blend-of-models`, `gfs-ensemble-forecast-system` | CDN-fronted; ~3–5ms connect anywhere; TTFB still ~110–290ms depending on distance to US origin |\n112\t| NOMADS | `nomads.ncep.noaa.gov` | CDN-fronted HTTP; index + range-request capable; good TTFB fallback |\n113\t\n114\tThe optimal provider is **per-model and per-deployment** — e.g. a Europe-based host\n115\tshould prefer GCS for HRRR + NBM (CDN gives 3ms connect vs 100ms S3) while a\n116\tUS-east host should prefer S3 for GFS + NBM (direct 11ms connect, 375ms TTFB vs\n117\tGCS's 112ms). Hardcoding a provider is fragile; instead, gribtract probes at\n118\tstartup and caches the result.\n119\t\n120\t**Probe mechanism (`xtask probe-providers` / runtime `ProviderProbe`):**\n121\t\n122\t1. For each (model, provider) pair, issue a small representative request:\n123\t   a. Fetch the `.idx` index file (a few KB, always text) for a recent known cycle.\n124\t   b. Issue one HTTP range request for the first message in that index (validates\n125\t      both connectivity and range-request support end-to-end).\n126\t2. Record: TCP connect time, TTFB, bytes-per-second for the range payload.\n127\t3. Rank providers per model by a combined score: `connect_ms + ttfb_ms + 1000/throughput_mbs`.\n128\t4. Write results to `provider-probe.json` (alongside `bench-results.json`).\n129\t5. At runtime, the fetch layer loads `provider-probe.json` on startup and falls\n130\t   back to re-probing live if the file is absent, stale (>24h), or if a provider\n131\t   returns HTTP errors on N consecutive requests.\n132\t\n133\t**Probe is non-blocking for decoding.** The library itself (gribtract-core,\n134\tgribtract) has zero network dependencies. The probe lives in `xtask` and in an\n135\toptional `gribtract-fetch` crate that the CLI and downstream integrations opt in to.\n136\t\n137\t### Fixed-station point-extraction benchmark\n138\tA second benchmark workload alongside bulk full-grid decode: extract a per-station\n139\t**time series** (one value per forecast hour, across a cycle) at a fixed roster of\n140\t20 US metropolitan METAR station coordinates — the common \"I only need the value at\n141\tthese points, across the horizon\" hot path. It measures `stations × hours / sec`\n142\tfrom raw bytes to assembled matrix, in nearest and bilinear modes, each verified\n143\tagainst the full-grid decode at the same points. This is deliberately an **open-\n144\tended optimization target**: decoding a whole grid to read a few points is wasteful,\n145\tbut GRIB2 packing makes cheap random access hard, so there is a long tail of\n146\ttechniques to try — many will be dead ends, and that is expected. See\n147\t`docs/notes/station-extraction-benchmark.md`.\n148\t\n149\t## Data Models\n150\t\n151\t```rust\n152\tstruct Message<'a> { sections: Sections<'a>, fields: Vec<Field> }\n153\t\n154\tstruct Field {\n155\t    discipline: u8,\n156\t    parameter: ParameterId,      // category + number → name/units\n157\t    level: Level,                // type + value(s)\n158\t    forecast: ForecastTime,      // reference time + offset + statistical window\n159\t    ensemble: Option<Ensemble>,  // member / type\n160\t    grid: GridDefinition,        // template 3.x → geometry + point order\n161\t    values: GridValues,          // f64 grid + missing mask\n162\t}\n163\t\n164\tenum GridValues { Dense(Vec<f64>), Masked { values: Vec<f64>, present: BitVec } }\n165\t```\n166\t\n167\t### Benchmark result (drives the dashboard)\n168\t\n169\t```jsonc\n170\t// bench-results.json — one record per benchmarked decode run\n171\t{\n172\t  \"git_sha\": \"…\", \"timestamp\": \"…\",          // provenance, stamped by xtask\n173\t  \"host\": { \"cpu\": \"…\", \"cores\": 8, \"mem_gb\": 62 },\n174\t  \"corpus\": { \"name\": \"gfs-2026-06\", \"messages\": 4096, \"bytes\": 1734967296 },\n175\t  \"runs\": [\n176\t    { \"decoder\": \"gribtract\", \"template_5x\": \"5.3\",\n177\t      \"messages_per_sec\": 0, \"mb_per_sec\": 0, \"grid_points_per_sec\": 0,\n178\t      \"wall_ms\": 0, \"agreement\": 1.0 },          // correctness rides alongside speed\n179\t    { \"decoder\": \"eccodes\",  \"template_5x\": \"5.3\", \"mb_per_sec\": 0, \"wall_ms\": 0 },\n180\t    { \"decoder\": \"wgrib2\",   \"template_5x\": \"5.3\", \"mb_per_sec\": 0, \"wall_ms\": 0 }\n181\t  ]\n182\t}\n183\t```\n184\t\n185\tSpeedup vs. each reference decoder is computed from these records; the dashboard\n186\trenders the comparison and the absolute throughput, both tagged with `git_sha` +\n187\t`host` so a claimed number is always reproducible and attributable.\n188\t\n189\t## Implementation Phases\n190\t\n191\t- [x] **Phase 0 — Oracle harness first.** Stand up the corpus loader, the\n192\t  eccodes/wgrib2 reference runner (on a host with the reference toolchain installed),\n193\t  the field-by-field comparator, and the tolerance policy. Wire `cargo test` to run\n194\t  a sampled differential suite. *Deliverable: a failing-but-green-framework that\n195\t  can score any decoder against the corpus.*\n196\t- [x] **Phase 1 — Framing + metadata.** Section 0–8 split, identification/grid/\n197\t  product metadata. Match all non-value fields exactly for GFS surface temp.\n198\t- [x] **Phase 2 — Simple packing (5.0) + lat/lon grid (3.0).** First end-to-end\n199\t  numeric agreement on the most common GFS/NBM fields.\n200\t- [x] **Phase 2b — Proof-of-speed dashboard (wired in here, updated forever after).**\n201\t  Benchmark harness emits `bench-results.json` (throughput + agreement, vs\n202\t  eccodes/wgrib2); self-contained HTML dashboard renders it with a live `--serve`\n203\t  mode. From here on, every phase's work must keep the dashboard green and current.\n204\t  See `docs/notes/throughput-dashboard.md`.\n205\t- [ ] **Phase 2c — Point-extraction parse-speed track (ongoing, never \"done\").**\n206\t  Add the fixed-station point-extraction benchmark (`stations × hours / sec`,\n207\t  nearest + bilinear, each verified against full-grid decode at the points), then\n208\t  **iterate on parse speed indefinitely** — lazy/partial unpack, geometry +\n209\t  bitmap-rank caches reused across forecast hours, SIMD spans, decode-once-extract-\n210\t  many, parallelism, zero-copy slicing, and techniques not yet thought of. **Expect\n211\t  many dead ends and failed attempts — that is the point of the track.** A technique\n212\t  that proves *not* to help is a valid iteration: record it in the techniques log so\n213\t  the loop doesn't re-walk it. The only hard rule is the project's rule — correctness\n214\t  gates speed; a faster path that changes a value is a regression, not progress.\n215\t  See `docs/notes/station-extraction-benchmark.md`. This track runs in parallel with\n216\t  the phases below and is never checked off.\n217\t- [x] **Phase 3 — Complex packing (5.2/5.3) + spatial differencing.** Unlocks most\n218\t  HRRR fields. The numerically trickiest unpacker.\n219\t- [x] **Phase 4 — JPEG2000 (5.40) + PNG (5.41).** Compressed grids (common in some\n220\t  NOAA products). Pull in a Rust j2k/png path.\n221\t- [x] **Phase 5 — Grid geometry breadth.** Lambert conformal (HRRR native), polar\n222\t  stereographic, Gaussian, rotated. Geometry must match for point asof-joins.\n223\t- [x] **Phase 6 — Ensembles + statistical products (GEFS).** Product templates for\n224\t  members and time-aggregated fields.\n225\t- [x] **Phase 7 — Publish + integrate.** crates.io, Python bindings, and the\n226\t  forecast-timeseries emitter consumed by downstream time-series analysis.\n227\t  Includes the **provider probe** (`xtask probe-providers` + runtime `ProviderProbe`):\n228\t  each candidate provider for each model is probed at startup (`.idx` fetch + one\n229\t  range request); results cached to `provider-probe.json` with a 24h TTL. See the\n230\t  \"Provider probe & selection\" component above for the full spec.\n231\t\n232\t## Marathon loop contract\n233\t\n234\tFor an autonomous session to make safe, monotonic progress, each iteration must:\n235\t1. Pick a corpus message currently failing (or absent) — the harness lists them.\n236\t2. Add/confirm it as a fixture (real file, provenance recorded).\n237\t3. Implement/repair the relevant template decoder.\n238\t4. `cargo test` → full sampled differential suite passes; no regression vs the\n239\t   ratcheted fixture set.\n240\t5. Commit. The loop drives two coupled north-star metrics upward, **in this\n241\t   priority**: (a) **agreement coverage** (% messages matching, by template) — the\n242\t   gate; and (b) **decode throughput** (MB/s and speedup vs eccodes/wgrib2, plus\n243\t   `stations × hours / sec` for point extraction) — the proof. A throughput gain\n244\t   that lowers agreement is a regression, not progress. Both are published to the\n245\t   proof-of-speed dashboard every iteration.\n246\t\n247\tSeparately from the phase work, the **point-extraction parse-speed track (Phase 2c)\n248\tis an open-ended optimization loop**: keep trying new techniques to make station\n249\textraction faster, expect most attempts to fail or regress and back them out, and\n250\tlog what didn't work so the loop doesn't repeat it. Dead ends are normal here — the\n251\tcorrectness gate makes the exploration safe. When no template/correctness bead is\n252\tthe obvious next pick, advancing this track is always valid work.\n253\t\n254\t## Open Questions\n255\t\n256\t- **Reference decoder of record:** eccodes (richer metadata) vs wgrib2 (ubiquitous\n257\t  in NOAA tooling)? Likely eccodes primary, wgrib2 as a second cross-check.\n258\t- **Tolerance derivation:** confirm that decimal/binary scale factors fully\n259\t  determine representable precision for each packing template, or whether some\n260\t  templates need an empirically-set band.\n261\t- **Corpus governance:** fixtures are real grib2 — size/licensing/storage. Store\n262\t  large fixtures out-of-tree (B2) with a manifest + hash, fetched by the harness?\n263\t- **JPEG2000 in Rust:** is there a pure-Rust J2K decoder of sufficient fidelity,\n264\t  or is a vetted FFI dependency acceptable for template 5.40 only?\n265\t- **Where the reference runs:** the differential harness needs the C toolchain;\n266\t  run it on a host with the reference toolchain installed. CI wiring TBD.\n267\t- **Scope of metadata naming:** ship a parameter/level name table, or expose raw\n268\t  numeric ids and leave naming to the consumer initially?\n269\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"7ded7c84-77f0-44a9-b04e-d4b3c7653976","uuid":"062e7c9b-ccde-4866-b067-e1060693018b","timestamp":"2026-07-02T14:41:20.049Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/gribtract/docs/plan/plan.md","content":"# gribtract Plan\n\n## Overview\n\nA pure-Rust GRIB2 decoder that turns NOAA/WMO GRIB2 messages into typed metadata\nand gridded numeric data, gated at every step on field-by-field agreement with a\nreference decoder (eccodes / wgrib2) over a corpus of real NOAA products.\n\nThis is an **oracle-first** project. The differential harness is built before the\ndecoder is \"done,\" so that autonomous (marathon) coding sessions always have a\nconcrete, externally-verifiable hill to climb: *find a message gribtract decodes\nwrong → add it as a fixture → fix the decode → harness confirms no regression →\nrepeat.*\n\n## Design principles\n\n1. **The oracle is the product.** The decoder is only as trustworthy as the\n   corpus and the differential check behind it. Harness work is never \"overhead.\"\n2. **Real files only.** Fixtures are actual NOAA grib2 messages (GFS, HRRR, NBM,\n   GEFS, …), not synthetic. An upstream archive already captures these — seed from it.\n3. **Tolerance is derived, not guessed.** Grid-value equality is checked within\n   the precision implied by the packing template (e.g. decimal/binary scale\n   factors), not a magic epsilon. The tolerance policy is documented and tested.\n4. **Every disagreement becomes a permanent fixture.** Monotonic improvement; the\n   regression ratchet is the whole point.\n5. **Sub-minute verify.** `cargo test` (local, cgroup-limited or CI) must run\n   the full differential suite fast enough to drive an unattended loop. Large\n   corpora are sharded/sampled in the fast path, exhaustive in CI.\n\n## Architecture\n\n```\n                 ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n   .grib2 bytes  │  gribtract-core (no_std-friendly where able)  │\n   ───────────►  │                                                │\n                 │  Indicator(0) → Identification(1) → Local(2)   │\n                 │   → Grid Def(3) → Product Def(4)               │\n                 │   → Data Representation(5) → Bitmap(6)          │\n                 │   → Data(7) → End(8)                           │\n                 │                                                │\n                 │  section split → template dispatch → unpack    │\n                 └───────────────┬───────────────────────────────┘\n                                 │ Vec<Field { meta, grid, values }>\n                 ┌───────────────▼───────────────┐\n                 │  gribtract (high-level API)    │  iterate messages,\n                 │  + CLI (`gribtract decode`)    │  select by key, dump JSON\n                 └───────────────┬───────────────┘\n                                 │\n          ┌──────────────────────▼───────────────────────┐\n          │  differential harness (tests/ + xtask)         │\n          │  gribtract vs eccodes/wgrib2 over corpus       │\n          │  → field-by-field + grid-within-tolerance      │\n          └────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n```\n\n### Crate layout (workspace)\n\n- `crates/gribtract-core` — section parsing, template dispatch, unpacking. No I/O.\n- `crates/gribtract` — high-level message iterator, field selection, public API.\n- `crates/gribtract-cli` — `gribtract decode|list|dump` (JSON/CSV out).\n- `crates/gribtract-testutil` — shared test utilities and fixtures for differential testing.\n- `crates/gribtract-py` — Python bindings via PyO3.\n- `crates/gribtract-fetch` — HTTP byte-range fetching for GRIB2 files with provider probing.\n- `xtask` — corpus management, reference-decoder runner, tolerance reports.\n- `crates/gribtract/tests/` — differential conformance suite (differential.rs, station_extraction.rs).\n\n## Components\n\n### Section parser\nSplits a GRIB2 message into sections 0–8, validates length/number fields, supports\nmulti-field messages (repeated sections 2–7) and the GRIB2 \"edition 2\" framing.\n\n### Template registry\nGRIB2's complexity lives in three template families. Each gets a registry mapping\ntemplate number → decoder:\n- **Grid Definition Templates (3.x)** — lat/lon, Lambert conformal, polar\n  stereographic, Gaussian, rotated grids. Determines point geometry.\n- **Product Definition Templates (4.x)** — what the field *is* (parameter, level,\n  forecast time, ensemble member, statistical processing).\n- **Data Representation Templates (5.x)** — how values are packed: simple, complex,\n  complex+spatial-differencing, IEEE float, JPEG2000, PNG.\n\n### Unpackers\nThe hard numeric core. Per data-representation template: read reference value,\nbinary/decimal scale, bit-depth; expand bitmap; apply scaling; (for 5.40/5.41)\nJPEG2000/PNG decode; (for 5.3) spatial differencing reversal.\n\n### Differential harness\nRuns both decoders over the corpus, compares metadata exactly and grid values\nwithin derived tolerance, emits a per-template coverage + agreement report.\n\n### Throughput & proof-of-speed dashboard\nA self-contained HTML dashboard that **proves gribtract decodes at the claimed\nspeed, on real files, reproducibly** — and shows it next to correctness so speed\nis never claimed without proof of a correct decode. Fed by a machine-readable\n`bench-results.json` emitted by the benchmark harness (`cargo run --bin xtask -- bench`),\nnever hand-edited. See `docs/notes/throughput-dashboard.md` for the full spec.\nThis is the artifact that answers \"is it actually working at that speed?\" — it is\na deliverable, not an afterthought, and is wired in early (Phase 2) so every later\niteration updates it.\n\n### Provider probe & selection\n\ngribtract's fetch layer (Phase 7) must retrieve GRIB2 files from one of several\nupstream providers that mirror the same NOAA data with different latency and\nthroughput characteristics depending on deployment location:\n\n| Provider | Hosts | Notes |\n|----------|-------|-------|\n| NOAA S3 | `noaa-gfs-bdp-pds`, `noaa-hrrr-bdp-pds`, `noaa-gefs-pds`, `noaa-nbm-grib2-pds` (all us-east-1) | Direct regional endpoint; no CDN; ~10ms from US east coast, ~100ms from Europe |\n| Google Cloud Storage | `high-resolution-rapid-refresh`, `national-blend-of-models`, `gfs-ensemble-forecast-system` | CDN-fronted; ~3–5ms connect anywhere; TTFB still ~110–290ms depending on distance to US origin |\n| NOMADS | `nomads.ncep.noaa.gov` | CDN-fronted HTTP; index + range-request capable; good TTFB fallback |\n\nThe optimal provider is **per-model and per-deployment** — e.g. a Europe-based host\nshould prefer GCS for HRRR + NBM (CDN gives 3ms connect vs 100ms S3) while a\nUS-east host should prefer S3 for GFS + NBM (direct 11ms connect, 375ms TTFB vs\nGCS's 112ms). Hardcoding a provider is fragile; instead, gribtract probes at\nstartup and caches the result.\n\n**Probe mechanism (`xtask probe-providers` / runtime `ProviderProbe`):**\n\n1. For each (model, provider) pair, issue a small representative request:\n   a. Fetch the `.idx` index file (a few KB, always text) for a recent known cycle.\n   b. Issue one HTTP range request for the first message in that index (validates\n      both connectivity and range-request support end-to-end).\n2. Record: TCP connect time, TTFB, bytes-per-second for the range payload.\n3. Rank providers per model by a combined score: `connect_ms + ttfb_ms + 1000/throughput_mbs`.\n4. Write results to `provider-probe.json` (alongside `bench-results.json`).\n5. At runtime, the fetch layer loads `provider-probe.json` on startup and falls\n   back to re-probing live if the file is absent, stale (>24h), or if a provider\n   returns HTTP errors on N consecutive requests.\n\n**Probe is non-blocking for decoding.** The library itself (gribtract-core,\ngribtract) has zero network dependencies. The probe lives in `xtask` and in an\noptional `gribtract-fetch` crate that the CLI and downstream integrations opt in to.\n\n### Fixed-station point-extraction benchmark\nA second benchmark workload alongside bulk full-grid decode: extract a per-station\n**time series** (one value per forecast hour, across a cycle) at a fixed roster of\n20 US metropolitan METAR station coordinates — the common \"I only need the value at\nthese points, across the horizon\" hot path. It measures `stations × hours / sec`\nfrom raw bytes to assembled matrix, in nearest and bilinear modes, each verified\nagainst the full-grid decode at the same points. This is deliberately an **open-\nended optimization target**: decoding a whole grid to read a few points is wasteful,\nbut GRIB2 packing makes cheap random access hard, so there is a long tail of\ntechniques to try — many will be dead ends, and that is expected. See\n`docs/notes/station-extraction-benchmark.md`.\n\n## Data Models\n\n```rust\nstruct Message<'a> { sections: Sections<'a>, fields: Vec<Field> }\n\nstruct Field {\n    discipline: u8,\n    parameter: ParameterId,      // category + number → name/units\n    level: Level,                // type + value(s)\n    forecast: ForecastTime,      // reference time + offset + statistical window\n    ensemble: Option<Ensemble>,  // member / type\n    grid: GridDefinition,        // template 3.x → geometry + point order\n    values: GridValues,          // f64 grid + missing mask\n}\n\nenum GridValues { Dense(Vec<f64>), Masked { values: Vec<f64>, present: BitVec } }\n```\n\n### Benchmark result (drives the dashboard)\n\n```jsonc\n// bench-results.json — one record per benchmarked decode run\n{\n  \"git_sha\": \"…\", \"timestamp\": \"…\",          // provenance, stamped by xtask\n  \"host\": { \"cpu\": \"…\", \"cores\": 8, \"mem_gb\": 62 },\n  \"corpus\": { \"name\": \"gfs-2026-06\", \"messages\": 4096, \"bytes\": 1734967296 },\n  \"runs\": [\n    { \"decoder\": \"gribtract\", \"template_5x\": \"5.3\",\n      \"messages_per_sec\": 0, \"mb_per_sec\": 0, \"grid_points_per_sec\": 0,\n      \"wall_ms\": 0, \"agreement\": 1.0 },          // correctness rides alongside speed\n    { \"decoder\": \"eccodes\",  \"template_5x\": \"5.3\", \"mb_per_sec\": 0, \"wall_ms\": 0 },\n    { \"decoder\": \"wgrib2\",   \"template_5x\": \"5.3\", \"mb_per_sec\": 0, \"wall_ms\": 0 }\n  ]\n}\n```\n\nSpeedup vs. each reference decoder is computed from these records; the dashboard\nrenders the comparison and the absolute throughput, both tagged with `git_sha` +\n`host` so a claimed number is always reproducible and attributable.\n\n## Implementation Phases\n\n- [x] **Phase 0 — Oracle harness first.** Stand up the corpus loader, the\n  eccodes/wgrib2 reference runner (on a host with the reference toolchain installed),\n  the field-by-field comparator, and the tolerance policy. Wire `cargo test` to run\n  a sampled differential suite. *Deliverable: a failing-but-green-framework that\n  can score any decoder against the corpus.*\n- [x] **Phase 1 — Framing + metadata.** Section 0–8 split, identification/grid/\n  product metadata. Match all non-value fields exactly for GFS surface temp.\n- [x] **Phase 2 — Simple packing (5.0) + lat/lon grid (3.0).** First end-to-end\n  numeric agreement on the most common GFS/NBM fields.\n- [x] **Phase 2b — Proof-of-speed dashboard (wired in here, updated forever after).**\n  Benchmark harness emits `bench-results.json` (throughput + agreement, vs\n  eccodes/wgrib2); self-contained HTML dashboard renders it with a live `--serve`\n  mode. From here on, every phase's work must keep the dashboard green and current.\n  See `docs/notes/throughput-dashboard.md`.\n- [ ] **Phase 2c — Point-extraction parse-speed track (ongoing, never \"done\").**\n  Add the fixed-station point-extraction benchmark (`stations × hours / sec`,\n  nearest + bilinear, each verified against full-grid decode at the points), then\n  **iterate on parse speed indefinitely** — lazy/partial unpack, geometry +\n  bitmap-rank caches reused across forecast hours, SIMD spans, decode-once-extract-\n  many, parallelism, zero-copy slicing, and techniques not yet thought of. **Expect\n  many dead ends and failed attempts — that is the point of the track.** A technique\n  that proves *not* to help is a valid iteration: record it in the techniques log so\n  the loop doesn't re-walk it. The only hard rule is the project's rule — correctness\n  gates speed; a faster path that changes a value is a regression, not progress.\n  See `docs/notes/station-extraction-benchmark.md`. This track runs in parallel with\n  the phases below and is never checked off.\n- [x] **Phase 3 — Complex packing (5.2/5.3) + spatial differencing.** Unlocks most\n  HRRR fields. The numerically trickiest unpacker.\n- [x] **Phase 4 — JPEG2000 (5.40) + PNG (5.41).** Compressed grids (common in some\n  NOAA products). Pull in a Rust j2k/png path.\n- [x] **Phase 5 — Grid geometry breadth.** Lambert conformal (HRRR native), polar\n  stereographic, Gaussian, rotated. Geometry must match for point asof-joins.\n- [x] **Phase 6 — Ensembles + statistical products (GEFS).** Product templates for\n  members and time-aggregated fields.\n- [x] **Phase 7 — Publish + integrate.** crates.io, Python bindings, and the\n  forecast-timeseries emitter consumed by downstream time-series analysis.\n  Includes the **provider probe** (`xtask probe-providers` + runtime `ProviderProbe`):\n  each candidate provider for each model is probed at startup (`.idx` fetch + one\n  range request); results cached to `provider-probe.json` with a 24h TTL. See the\n  \"Provider probe & selection\" component above for the full spec.\n\n## Marathon loop contract\n\nFor an autonomous session to make safe, monotonic progress, each iteration must:\n1. Pick a corpus message currently failing (or absent) — the harness lists them.\n2. Add/confirm it as a fixture (real file, provenance recorded).\n3. Implement/repair the relevant template decoder.\n4. `cargo test` → full sampled differential suite passes; no regression vs the\n   ratcheted fixture set.\n5. Commit. The loop drives two coupled north-star metrics upward, **in this\n   priority**: (a) **agreement coverage** (% messages matching, by template) — the\n   gate; and (b) **decode throughput** (MB/s and speedup vs eccodes/wgrib2, plus\n   `stations × hours / sec` for point extraction) — the proof. A throughput gain\n   that lowers agreement is a regression, not progress. Both are published to the\n   proof-of-speed dashboard every iteration.\n\nSeparately from the phase work, the **point-extraction parse-speed track (Phase 2c)\nis an open-ended optimization loop**: keep trying new techniques to make station\nextraction faster, expect most attempts to fail or regress and back them out, and\nlog what didn't work so the loop doesn't repeat it. Dead ends are normal here — the\ncorrectness gate makes the exploration safe. When no template/correctness bead is\nthe obvious next pick, advancing this track is always valid work.\n\n## Open Questions\n\n- **Reference decoder of record:** eccodes (richer metadata) vs wgrib2 (ubiquitous\n  in NOAA tooling)? Likely eccodes primary, wgrib2 as a second cross-check.\n- **Tolerance derivation:** confirm that decimal/binary scale factors fully\n  determine representable precision for each packing template, or whether some\n  templates need an empirically-set band.\n- **Corpus governance:** fixtures are real grib2 — size/licensing/storage. Store\n  large fixtures out-of-tree (B2) with a manifest + hash, fetched by the harness?\n- **JPEG2000 in Rust:** is there a pure-Rust J2K decoder of sufficient fidelity,\n  or is a vetted FFI dependency acceptable for template 5.40 only?\n- **Where the reference runs:** the differential harness needs the C toolchain;\n  run it on a host with the reference toolchain installed. CI wiring TBD.\n- **Scope of metadata naming:** ship a parameter/level name table, or expose raw\n  numeric ids and leave naming to the consumer initially?\n","numLines":269,"startLine":1,"totalLines":269}}}

It models the\n4\textremely common point-forecast use case: **\"I don't need the whole grid — I need\n5\tthe value at a fixed roster of station coordinates, across the whole forecast\n6\thorizon.\"** This is the parse-speed hot path most downstream consumers actually hit.\n7\t\n8\t## The workload\n9\t\n10\t- **Input:** one model cycle's worth of messages for a single field (e.g. 2 m\n11\t  temperature), one message per forecast hour — `f000, f003, … f120`.\n12\t- **Task:** for a fixed roster of ~7–10 US metropolitan weather-station points,\n13\t  produce a per-station **time series**: the field value at that point for every\n14\t  forecast hour. Nearest-grid-point and bilinear-interpolation variants both timed.\n15\t- **Output the benchmark measures:** wall time and throughput to go from raw\n16\t  `.grib2` bytes → the assembled `stations × hours` matrix.\n17\t\n18\t### Station roster (fixed, representative US metros)\n19\t\n20\tA stable set of major-metro METAR station coordinates so runs are comparable over\n21\ttime. geographic probe points for a reproducible point-extraction benchmark,\n47\tcovering a broad spread of US grid regions — coastal, interior, high-elevation,\n48\tlow-latitude, and all four CONUS time zones.\n49\t\n50\t## Why this is a rich optimization target\n51\t\n52\tDecoding the *entire* grid to read 7 points is hugely wasteful, but GRIB2 packing\n53\tmakes random point access hard — so this is exactly the kind of problem with a long\n54\ttail of techniques to try, many of which will be **dead ends**. That is expected and\n55\tfine; the correctness gate + dashboard keep every attempt honest. Candidate angles\n56\t(non-exhaustive, the marathon should invent more):\n57\t\n58\t- **Lazy / partial unpack** — for simple packing (5.0) the value at point *i* is a\n59\t  pure function of the packed bits at offset *i*; decode only the cells covering the\n60\t  target points instead of the full grid.\n61\t- **Bitmap-aware indexing** — when a bitmap is present, point→packed-index needs a\n62\t  rank/select over the bitmap; precompute it once per grid geometry, reuse across\n63\t  forecast hours (the grid is identical for every hour in a cycle).\n64\t- **Geometry cache** — lat/lon → grid (i,j) is the same for all messages on the same\n65\t  grid; compute the station→index map once, reuse for the whole time series.\n66\t- **SIMD unpack** of the bit-packed integers for the spans that contain targets.\n67\t- **Decode-once-extract-many** — amortize section parsing across all stations.\n68\t- **Parallelism** — fan the per-forecast-hour messages across cores.\n69\t- **Zero-copy** section slicing to avoid per-message allocation.\n70\t- **Complex packing (5.2/5.3) reality check** — grouped/spatial-differenced data may\n71\t  not allow cheap random access; partial decode may be impossible and the honest\n72\t  answer is \"fall back to full decode for these templates.\" Proving a technique\n73\t  *doesn't* work and recording why is a valid, valuable iteration.\n74\t\n75\t## Honesty rules (same as the rest of the project)\n76\t\n77\t- **Correctness gates speed.** Every extracted time series is checked against the\n78\t  full-grid decode (and, where available, the reference decoder) at the same points,\n79\t  within the derived tolerance. A faster path that changes a value is a regression.\n80\t- **Interpolation is explicit.** Nearest-point and bilinear are separate, separately\n81\t  verified modes — never silently swapped to win a benchmark.\n82\t- **Published to the dashboard.** Station-extraction runs emit their own\n83\t  `bench-results.json` records (`workload: \"station-extract\"`) with `git_sha` +\n84\t  `host`, so the `stations × hours / sec` trend is tracked across commits next to\n85\t  its agreement check — a speed number is never shown without its correctness proof.\n86\t\n87\t## Definition of done (for the benchmark itself; the *optimization* never \"finishes\")\n88\t\n89\t- [x] `xtask bench --workload station-extract` builds the per-station time series and\n90\t  emits throughput + agreement records.\n91\t- [x] Nearest and bilinear modes, each verified against full-grid decode at the points.\n92\t- [ ] Dashboard view: `stations × hours / sec` over commits, with the agreement\n93\t  companion.\n94\t- [x] A short `notes/` running log of techniques tried — including the ones that\n95\t  *didn't* help and why — so the loop doesn't re-walk dead ends.\n96\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"7ded7c84-77f0-44a9-b04e-d4b3c7653976","uuid":"ab112f28-879b-4c61-9f40-0b5fc83d54dd","timestamp":"2026-07-02T14:41:35.486Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/gribtract/docs/notes/station-extraction-benchmark.md","content":"# Fixed-Station Point-Extraction Benchmark\n\nA second benchmark workload, distinct from the bulk full-grid decode. It models the\nextremely common point-forecast use case: **\"I don't need the whole grid — I need\nthe value at a fixed roster of station coordinates, across the whole forecast\nhorizon.\"** This is the parse-speed hot path most downstream consumers actually hit.\n\n## The workload\n\n- **Input:** one model cycle's worth of messages for a single field (e.g. 2 m\n  temperature), one message per forecast hour — `f000, f003, … f120`.\n- **Task:** for a fixed roster of ~7–10 US metropolitan weather-station points,\n  produce a per-station **time series**: the field value at that point for every\n  forecast hour. Nearest-grid-point and bilinear-interpolation variants both timed.\n- **Output the benchmark measures:** wall time and throughput to go from raw\n  `.grib2` bytes → the assembled `stations × hours` matrix.\n\n### Station roster (fixed, representative US metros)\n\nA stable set of major-metro METAR station coordinates so runs are comparable over\ntime. Coordinates match official observation sites for reproducibility.\n\n| Station | METAR | Lat | Lon | TZ |\n|---------|-------|-----|-----|----|\n| New York, NY (Central Park) | KNYC | 40.7789 | −73.9692 | ET |\n| Miami, FL                   | KMIA | 25.7959 | −80.2870 | ET |\n| Philadelphia, PA            | KPHL | 39.8721 | −75.2411 | ET |\n| Atlanta, GA                 | KATL | 33.6407 | −84.4277 | ET |\n| Boston, MA                  | KBOS | 42.3656 | −71.0096 | ET |\n| Washington, DC              | KDCA | 38.8512 | −77.0402 | ET |\n| Chicago, IL (Midway)        | KMDW | 41.7868 | −87.7522 | CT |\n| Dallas, TX                  | KDFW | 32.8998 | −97.0403 | CT |\n| Houston, TX                 | KIAH | 29.9902 | −95.3368 | CT |\n| Minneapolis, MN             | KMSP | 44.8820 | −93.2218 | CT |\n| Austin, TX                  | KAUS | 30.1945 | −97.6699 | CT |\n| New Orleans, LA             | KMSY | 29.9934 | −90.2580 | CT |\n| San Antonio, TX             | KSAT | 29.5337 | −98.4698 | CT |\n| Oklahoma City, OK           | KOKC | 35.3931 | −97.6007 | CT |\n| Denver, CO                  | KDEN | 39.8561 | −104.6737 | MT |\n| Phoenix, AZ                 | KPHX | 33.4373 | −112.0078 | AZ |\n| Los Angeles, CA             | KLAX | 33.9416 | −118.4085 | PT |\n| Las Vegas, NV               | KLAS | 36.0840 | −115.1537 | PT |\n| Seattle, WA                 | KSEA | 47.4502 | −122.3088 | PT |\n| San Francisco, CA           | KSFO | 37.6189 | −122.3750 | PT |\n\nThese are fixed geographic probe points for a reproducible point-extraction benchmark,\ncovering a broad spread of US grid regions — coastal, interior, high-elevation,\nlow-latitude, and all four CONUS time zones.\n\n## Why this is a rich optimization target\n\nDecoding the *entire* grid to read 7 points is hugely wasteful, but GRIB2 packing\nmakes random point access hard — so this is exactly the kind of problem with a long\ntail of techniques to try, many of which will be **dead ends**. That is expected and\nfine; the correctness gate + dashboard keep every attempt honest. Candidate angles\n(non-exhaustive, the marathon should invent more):\n\n- **Lazy / partial unpack** — for simple packing (5.0) the value at point *i* is a\n  pure function of the packed bits at offset *i*; decode only the cells covering the\n  target points instead of the full grid.\n- **Bitmap-aware indexing** — when a bitmap is present, point→packed-index needs a\n  rank/select over the bitmap; precompute it once per grid geometry, reuse across\n  forecast hours (the grid is identical for every hour in a cycle).\n- **Geometry cache** — lat/lon → grid (i,j) is the same for all messages on the same\n  grid; compute the station→index map once, reuse for the whole time series.\n- **SIMD unpack** of the bit-packed integers for the spans that contain targets.\n- **Decode-once-extract-many** — amortize section parsing across all stations.\n- **Parallelism** — fan the per-forecast-hour messages across cores.\n- **Zero-copy** section slicing to avoid per-message allocation.\n- **Complex packing (5.2/5.3) reality check** — grouped/spatial-differenced data may\n  not allow cheap random access; partial decode may be impossible and the honest\n  answer is \"fall back to full decode for these templates.\" Proving a technique\n  *doesn't* work and recording why is a valid, valuable iteration.\n\n## Honesty rules (same as the rest of the project)\n\n- **Correctness gates speed.** Every extracted time series is checked against the\n  full-grid decode (and, where available, the reference decoder) at the same points,\n  within the derived tolerance. A faster path that changes a value is a regression.\n- **Interpolation is explicit.** Nearest-point and bilinear are separate, separately\n  verified modes — never silently swapped to win a benchmark.\n- **Published to the dashboard.** Station-extraction runs emit their own\n  `bench-results.json` records (`workload: \"station-extract\"`) with `git_sha` +\n  `host`, so the `stations × hours / sec` trend is tracked across commits next to\n  its agreement check — a speed number is never shown without its correctness proof.\n\n## Definition of done (for the benchmark itself; the *optimization* never \"finishes\")\n\n- [x] `xtask bench --workload station-extract` builds the per-station time series and\n  emits throughput + agreement records.\n- [x] Nearest and bilinear modes, each verified against full-grid decode at the points.\n- [ ] Dashboard view: `stations × hours / sec` over commits, with the agreement\n  companion.\n- [x] A short `notes/` running log of techniques tried — including the ones that\n  *didn't* help and why — so the loop doesn't re-walk dead ends.\n","numLines":96,"startLine":1,"totalLines":96}}}

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_7bfb6e356da54e7dab0560fb","type":"tool_result","content":"ID: bf-6bxh\nTitle: Station benchmark bilinear agreement is not a correctness gate (42.9% published to dashboard)\nStatus: closed\nPriority: P2\nType: bug\nDescription: The plan (Phase 2c) and docs/notes/station-extraction-benchmark.md require nearest AND bilinear modes \"each verified against full-grid decode at the points\". The current verify() in xtask/src/bench_station.rs (around line 591) instead compares the bilinear-interpolated value against the NEAREST grid-point value with a 4x-packing-step tolerance — the comment admits it verifies the value is \"physically reasonable, not to check equality\". Result: bilinear agreement is published as 42.9% (see docs/notes/parse-speed-log.md Attempt 3 and bench-results.json), which is meaningless as a gate and alarming on the dashboard, where agreement is supposed to be the proof that speed numbers are correct.\n\nWhat to do: verify the fast bilinear path against an independently computed reference bilinear at the same points — e.g. a naive straight-line reimplementation that takes the four corner values from the fully decoded grid (GridDefinition::bilinear_corners) and combines them with the same fractional weights, compared within derived tolerance. Bilinear-vs-bilinear should agree at ~100%; if it does not, that is a real bug in GridValues::bilinear (crates/gribtract-core/src/types.rs ~line 581) and must be fixed, not tolerated.\n\nAcceptance criteria:\n- verify() checks bilinear against a reference bilinear (equality within derived tolerance), not against the nearest value.\n- Published bilinear agreement is a true gate (expected 100%); any deficit is investigated and fixed.\n- The unchecked \"Nearest and bilinear modes... verified\" box in docs/notes/station-extraction-benchmark.md can be honestly ticked.\n- bench-results.json regenerated so the dashboard no longer shows the 42.9% figure.\nAssignee: claude-code-glm47-india","is_error":false}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"7ded7c84-77f0-44a9-b04e-d4b3c7653976","uuid":"a78a8c73-84f3-46f6-b697-e8abc7ed0200","timestamp":"2026-07-02T14:42:26.231Z","tool_use_result":{"stdout":"ID: bf-6bxh\nTitle: Station benchmark bilinear agreement is not a correctness gate (42.9% published to dashboard)\nStatus: closed\nPriority: P2\nType: bug\nDescription: The plan (Phase 2c) and docs/notes/station-extraction-benchmark.md require nearest AND bilinear modes \"each verified against full-grid decode at the points\". The current verify() in xtask/src/bench_station.rs (around line 591) instead compares the bilinear-interpolated value against the NEAREST grid-point value with a 4x-packing-step tolerance — the comment admits it verifies the value is \"physically reasonable, not to check equality\". Result: bilinear agreement is published as 42.9% (see docs/notes/parse-speed-log.md Attempt 3 and bench-results.json), which is meaningless as a gate and alarming on the dashboard, where agreement is supposed to be the proof that speed numbers are correct.\n\nWhat to do: verify the fast bilinear path against an independently computed reference bilinear at the same points — e.g. a naive straight-line reimplementation that takes the four corner values from the fully decoded grid (GridDefinition::bilinear_corners) and combines them with the same fractional weights, compared within derived tolerance. Bilinear-vs-bilinear should agree at ~100%; if it does not, that is a real bug in GridValues::bilinear (crates/gribtract-core/src/types.rs ~line 581) and must be fixed, not tolerated.\n\nAcceptance criteria:\n- verify() checks bilinear against a reference bilinear (equality within derived tolerance), not against the nearest value.\n- Published bilinear agreement is a true gate (expected 100%); any deficit is investigated and fixed.\n- The unchecked \"Nearest and bilinear modes... verified\" box in docs/notes/station-extraction-benchmark.md can be honestly ticked.\n- bench-results.json regenerated so the dashboard no longer shows the 42.9% figure.\nAssignee: claude-code-glm47-india","stderr":"","interrupted":false,"isImage":false,"noOutputExpected":false}}

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_b13476d3601543a0ad615a32","type":"tool_result","content":"The file /home/coding/gribtract/docs/notes/station-extraction-benchmark.md has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"7ded7c84-77f0-44a9-b04e-d4b3c7653976","uuid":"d255c0da-880b-4e4a-8a59-f4bd7b14931b","timestamp":"2026-07-02T14:43:14.565Z","tool_use_result":{"filePath":"/home/coding/gribtract/docs/notes/station-extraction-benchmark.md","oldString":"- [ ] Dashboard view: `stations × hours / sec` over commits, with the agreement\n  companion.","newString":"- [x] Dashboard view: `stations × hours / sec` over commits, with the agreement\n  companion.","originalFile":"# Fixed-Station Point-Extraction Benchmark\n\nA second benchmark workload, distinct from the bulk full-grid decode. It models the\nextremely common point-forecast use case: **\"I don't need the whole grid — I need\nthe value at a fixed roster of station coordinates, across the whole forecast\nhorizon.\"** This is the parse-speed hot path most downstream consumers actually hit.\n\n## The workload\n\n- **Input:** one model cycle's worth of messages for a single field (e.g. 2 m\n  temperature), one message per forecast hour — `f000, f003, … f120`.\n- **Task:** for a fixed roster of ~7–10 US metropolitan weather-station points,\n  produce a per-station **time series**: the field value at that point for every\n  forecast hour. Nearest-grid-point and bilinear-interpolation variants both timed.\n- **Output the benchmark measures:** wall time and throughput to go from raw\n  `.grib2` bytes → the assembled `stations × hours` matrix.\n\n### Station roster (fixed, representative US metros)\n\nA stable set of major-metro METAR station coordinates so runs are comparable over\ntime. Coordinates match official observation sites for reproducibility.\n\n| Station | METAR | Lat | Lon | TZ |\n|---------|-------|-----|-----|----|\n| New York, NY (Central Park) | KNYC | 40.7789 | −73.9692 | ET |\n| Miami, FL                   | KMIA | 25.7959 | −80.2870 | ET |\n| Philadelphia, PA            | KPHL | 39.8721 | −75.2411 | ET |\n| Atlanta, GA                 | KATL | 33.6407 | −84.4277 | ET |\n| Boston, MA                  | KBOS | 42.3656 | −71.0096 | ET |\n| Washington, DC              | KDCA | 38.8512 | −77.0402 | ET |\n| Chicago, IL (Midway)        | KMDW | 41.7868 | −87.7522 | CT |\n| Dallas, TX                  | KDFW | 32.8998 | −97.0403 | CT |\n| Houston, TX                 | KIAH | 29.9902 | −95.3368 | CT |\n| Minneapolis, MN             | KMSP | 44.8820 | −93.2218 | CT |\n| Austin, TX                  | KAUS | 30.1945 | −97.6699 | CT |\n| New Orleans, LA             | KMSY | 29.9934 | −90.2580 | CT |\n| San Antonio, TX             | KSAT | 29.5337 | −98.4698 | CT |\n| Oklahoma City, OK           | KOKC | 35.3931 | −97.6007 | CT |\n| Denver, CO                  | KDEN | 39.8561 | −104.6737 | MT |\n| Phoenix, AZ                 | KPHX | 33.4373 | −112.0078 | AZ |\n| Los Angeles, CA             | KLAX | 33.9416 | −118.4085 | PT |\n| Las Vegas, NV               | KLAS | 36.0840 | −115.1537 | PT |\n| Seattle, WA                 | KSEA | 47.4502 | −122.3088 | PT |\n| San Francisco, CA           | KSFO | 37.6189 | −122.3750 | PT |\n\nThese are fixed geographic probe points for a reproducible point-extraction benchmark,\ncovering a broad spread of US grid regions — coastal, interior, high-elevation,\nlow-latitude, and all four CONUS time zones.\n\n## Why this is a rich optimization target\n\nDecoding the *entire* grid to read 7 points is hugely wasteful, but GRIB2 packing\nmakes random point access hard — so this is exactly the kind of problem with a long\ntail of techniques to try, many of which will be **dead ends**. That is expected and\nfine; the correctness gate + dashboard keep every attempt honest. Candidate angles\n(non-exhaustive, the marathon should invent more):\n\n- **Lazy / partial unpack** — for simple packing (5.0) the value at point *i* is a\n  pure function of the packed bits at offset *i*; decode only the cells covering the\n  target points instead of the full grid.\n- **Bitmap-aware indexing** — when a bitmap is present, point→packed-index needs a\n  rank/select over the bitmap; precompute it once per grid geometry, reuse across\n  forecast hours (the grid is identical for every hour in a cycle).\n- **Geometry cache** — lat/lon → grid (i,j) is the same for all messages on the same\n  grid; compute the station→index map once, reuse for the whole time series.\n- **SIMD unpack** of the bit-packed integers for the spans that contain targets.\n- **Decode-once-extract-many** — amortize section parsing across all stations.\n- **Parallelism** — fan the per-forecast-hour messages across cores.\n- **Zero-copy** section slicing to avoid per-message allocation.\n- **Complex packing (5.2/5.3) reality check** — grouped/spatial-differenced data may\n  not allow cheap random access; partial decode may be impossible and the honest\n  answer is \"fall back to full decode for these templates.\" Proving a technique\n  *doesn't* work and recording why is a valid, valuable iteration.\n\n## Honesty rules (same as the rest of the project)\n\n- **Correctness gates speed.** Every extracted time series is checked against the\n  full-grid decode (and, where available, the reference decoder) at the same points,\n  within the derived tolerance. A faster path that changes a value is a regression.\n- **Interpolation is explicit.** Nearest-point and bilinear are separate, separately\n  verified modes — never silently swapped to win a benchmark.\n- **Published to the dashboard.** Station-extraction runs emit their own\n  `bench-results.json` records (`workload: \"station-extract\"`) with `git_sha` +\n  `host`, so the `stations × hours / sec` trend is tracked across commits next to\n  its agreement check — a speed number is never shown without its correctness proof.\n\n## Definition of done (for the benchmark itself; the *optimization* never \"finishes\")\n\n- [x] `xtask bench --workload station-extract` builds the per-station time series and\n  emits throughput + agreement records.\n- [x] Nearest and bilinear modes, each verified against full-grid decode at the points.\n- [ ] Dashboard view: `stations × hours / sec` over commits, with the agreement\n  companion.\n- [x] A short `notes/` running log of techniques tried — including the ones that\n  *didn't* help and why — so the loop doesn't re-walk dead ends.\n","structuredPatch":[{"oldStart":89,"oldLines":7,"newStart":89,"newLines":7,"lines":[" - [x] `xtask bench --workload station-extract` builds the per-station time series and","   emits throughput + agreement records."," - [x] Nearest and bilinear modes, each verified against full-grid decode at the points.","-- [ ] Dashboard view: `stations × hours / sec` over commits, with the agreement","+- [x] Dashboard view: `stations × hours / sec` over commits, with the agreement","   companion."," - [x] A short `notes/` running log of techniques tried — including the ones that","   *didn't* help and why — so the loop doesn't re-walk dead ends."]}],"userModified":false,"replaceAll":false}}
